title: auto-update: support pods
body:
Support auto updating containers running inside pods.  Similar to
containers, the systemd units need to be generated via
`podman-generate-systemd --new $POD` to generate the pod's units.
 
Note that auto updating a container inside a pod will restart the entire
pod.  Updates of multiple containers inside a pod are batched, such that
a pod is restarted at most once.  That is effectively the same mechanism
for auto updating containers in a K8s YAML via the `podman-kube@`
template or via Quadlet.
 
Updating a single container unit without restarting the entire pod is
not possible.  The reasoning behind is that pods are created with
--exit-policy=stop which will render the pod to be stopped when auto
updating the only container inside the pod.  The (reverse) dependencies
between the pod and its containers unit have been carefully selected for
robustness.  Changes may entail undesired side effects or backward
incompatibilities that I am not comfortable with.
 
Fixes: #17181
Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <vrothberg@redhat.com> 

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Support auto updates for containers running inside a pod.
```

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *vrothberg* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). > Can you add a test for it?

I hoped I could sneak it in but I will have a look :)  The restart should be easier to test.

> Interestingly the units I have deployed on my home server do not contain the BindsTo=, I do not remember why I changed it but I think I likely wanted to restart a single container without the pods so this change looks good to me. Although I wonder if this could break some user expectations, I think at least you release note message should be more clear because and mention the exact change.

I was thinking about it but fortunately found the get-free-out-of-jail-ticket in the docs:
> Note  systemctl should only be used on the pod unit and one should not start or stop containers individually via systemctl, as they are managed by the pod service along with the internal infra-container.

So we never gave a guarantee on the reverse dependency being stable.  Unfortunately, I don't see another way to fix the auto update issue.

Yes I am totally on board with this change but the release note should be more clear,
how about: 
```
Removed the `BindsTo=` option from the generated systemd units to support auto updating individual containers in pods with podman auto-update
```

I had to revise the initial version of the PR where I only relaxed the reverse dependencies.  Explanation is in the PR/commit message.

@Luap99 PTAL - as discussed yesterday.

>  Also hard coding the name will break portability.

The dependencies are already hard-coded, so I don't think that's an issue.

> This will break my passthrough logs 

That's a very fair point.  Updating the auto-update code to use the pod's unit instead sounds like a nice alternative :+1:
I'm seeing a test failure now, `image is in use by container`

> I'm seeing a test failure now, `image is in use by container`

Can you share which test is failing on your end?
I can't replicate; must've been a screwup on my end. And yes, CI is unwell.
rebase on main to get CI to pass. (Sorry, fix is only on main, not side branches)
